simple_explanation: To move from one place to another.
daily_life_use: Used when leaving a place or moving towards a destination.
examples: Let's go to the park.
Where did he go?
common_mistakes: Saying 'I go to home' instead of 'I go home'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi, we say 'à¤®à¥ˆà¤‚ à¤˜à¤° à¤œà¤¾ à¤°à¤¹à¤¾ à¤¹à¥‚à¤', in English 'I am going home' (no 'to').
formal_informal_note: Used in both formal and informal situations.

simple_explanation: To move toward the speaker or a specific place.
daily_life_use: Used when inviting someone or talking about arriving.
examples: Can you come to my party?
She came back late.
common_mistakes: Using 'come' when you mean 'go' (e.g., 'I am coming there' sounds unnatural if you mean going away).
hindi_confusion_correction: Remember 'come' is movement towards the speaker, 'go' is away.

simple_explanation: To move on foot at a normal speed.
daily_life_use: Going places by foot, or exercising.
examples: I walk to school.
We walked for two hours.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing the 'l' in walk. The 'l' is silent.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'à¤šà¤²à¤¨à¤¾' can mean 'go' or 'walk', but English 'walk' strictly means moving on foot.

simple_explanation: To move on foot faster than walking.
daily_life_use: Exercising or hurrying to catch something.
spoken_use: I have to run to the store.
natural_native_use: I gotta run! (meaning I have to leave quickly)
examples: The dog loves to run.
Don't run in the corridor.
common_mistakes: Using 'run' for machines in the wrong way (though 'the car is running' is correct).
hindi_confusion_correction: Can also mean managing something (e.g., 'run a business' - à¤¬à¤¿à¤œà¤¼à¤¨à¥‡à¤¸ à¤šà¤²à¤¾à¤¨à¤¾).

simple_explanation: To rest your body on a chair or the ground.
daily_life_use: Resting, working at a desk, or eating.
examples: Please sit down.
She was sitting on the floor.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'seat' (noun) and 'sit' (verb).
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Seat' is the object you sit on, 'Sit' is the action.

simple_explanation: To be on your feet in an upright position.
daily_life_use: Waiting in a line or getting up from a chair.
natural_native_use: I can't stand it (idiom meaning I hate it).
examples: Don't stand in the rain.
He was standing by the door.
common_mistakes: Saying 'standing on my head' when meaning 'upright'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Also used for tolerating ('à¤®à¥ˆà¤‚ à¤‡à¤¸à¥‡ à¤¸à¤¹ à¤¨à¤¹à¥€à¤‚ à¤¸à¤•à¤¤à¤¾' = 'I can't stand it').

simple_explanation: To push yourself off the ground into the air.
daily_life_use: Playing sports or avoiding an obstacle.
examples: The cat jumped onto the table.
Kids love to jump on the bed.
common_mistakes: Using jump when 'hop' or 'skip' is more accurate.
hindi_confusion_correction: Jump implies leaving the ground completely.

simple_explanation: To drop down towards the ground accidentally.
daily_life_use: Tripping over something or dropping items.
examples: He fell off his bike.
Leaves fall in autumn.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'fall' (intransitive) with 'drop' (transitive).
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤—à¤¿à¤°à¤¨à¤¾ = fall (happens to you), à¤—à¤¿à¤°à¤¾à¤¨à¤¾ = drop (you do it to an object).

simple_explanation: To come to an end or cause to not continue.
daily_life_use: Stopping a car, ending an activity.
natural_native_use: Can we stop by the store? (visit quickly)
examples: Please stop making noise.
The bus stopped at the station.
common_mistakes: Using 'stop to talk' vs 'stop talking' (they mean different things).
hindi_confusion_correction: Stop talking (à¤¬à¤¾à¤¤ à¤•à¤°à¤¨à¤¾ à¤¬à¤‚à¤¦ à¤•à¤°à¥‹), Stop to talk (à¤¬à¤¾à¤¤ à¤•à¤°à¤¨à¥‡ à¤•à¥‡ à¤²à¤¿à¤ à¤°à¥à¤•à¥‹).

simple_explanation: To stay in a place until an expected event happens.
daily_life_use: Waiting for a bus or a friend.
examples: I will wait for you outside.
She waited an hour for the train.
common_mistakes: Saying 'wait me' instead of 'wait for me'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Always use 'for' after wait when mentioning the object (Wait for me).

simple_explanation: To put food in your mouth and swallow it.
daily_life_use: Having meals like breakfast, lunch, or dinner.
examples: I eat an apple every day.
What did you eat for lunch?
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am eating water' instead of 'drinking water'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In English, 'eat' is only for solids; 'drink' or 'take' is for liquids and medicines.

simple_explanation: To take a liquid into your mouth and swallow it.
daily_life_use: Consuming water, tea, coffee, or juice.
spoken_use: I need a drink of water.
natural_native_use: Let's go grab a drink. (Often implies alcohol in native contexts)
examples: Make sure to drink plenty of water.
He is drinking tea.
common_mistakes: Using 'drink' for soup. We say 'eat soup' or 'have soup'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Drink' without an object often implies drinking alcohol in Western culture.

simple_explanation: To rest with your eyes closed and your mind inactive.
daily_life_use: Resting at night.
natural_native_use: I'm going to crash. / Hit the sack.
examples: I sleep for 8 hours every night.
The baby is sleeping.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I was sleep' instead of 'I was sleeping' or 'I was asleep'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Asleep is an adjective (I am asleep), sleep is the verb.

simple_explanation: To stop sleeping.
daily_life_use: Starting your day in the morning.
examples: I wake up at 6 AM.
Please wake me up early tomorrow.
common_mistakes: Saying 'awake me' instead of 'wake me up'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Wake up implies stopping sleep. Get up implies physically leaving the bed.

simple_explanation: To prepare food by heating it.
daily_life_use: Making meals in the kitchen.
examples: She cooks dinner every night.
He is learning to cook.
common_mistakes: Calling a person who cooks a 'cooker'. A cooker is the machine; the person is a 'cook'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Don't translate 'à¤–à¤¾à¤¨à¤¾ à¤¬à¤¨à¤¾à¤¨à¥‡ à¤µà¤¾à¤²à¤¾' to 'cooker'. Use 'cook' or 'chef'.

simple_explanation: To remove dirt or marks from something.
daily_life_use: Tidying up the house or washing items.
examples: I need to clean my room.
She is cleaning the windows.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'clean' (action/state) with 'clear' (empty of obstacles).
hindi_confusion_correction: Use clean for dirt, clear for clutter or obstacles.

simple_explanation: To clean with water and usually soap.
daily_life_use: Cleaning clothes, dishes, or hands.
natural_native_use: Do the washing up. (British for washing dishes)
examples: Please wash your hands before eating.
He washed his car on Sunday.
common_mistakes: Saying 'wash the house' instead of 'clean the house'. Wash strictly implies using water.
hindi_confusion_correction: Only use 'wash' when water is heavily involved.

simple_explanation: To clean dirt from the floor using a broom.
daily_life_use: Daily house cleaning chore.
natural_native_use: Give the floor a quick sweep.
examples: She swept the floor this morning.
He is sweeping the dust away.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'sweep' with 'mop' (which uses water).
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤à¤¾à¤¡à¤¼à¥‚ à¤²à¤—à¤¾à¤¨à¤¾ is sweep, à¤ªà¥‹à¤›à¤¾ à¤²à¤—à¤¾à¤¨à¤¾ is mop.

simple_explanation: To clean or dry a surface by rubbing it with a cloth.
daily_life_use: Cleaning tables, counters, or drying hands.
examples: Wipe your feet before coming in.
She wiped the tears from her face.
common_mistakes: Using 'clean' instead of 'wipe' when specifically referring to using a cloth on a small spill.
hindi_confusion_correction: Wipe specifically focuses on the rubbing action to remove liquid or dust.

simple_explanation: To use force to move something away from you.
daily_life_use: Opening doors, moving heavy objects.
examples: He pushed the heavy cart.
Don't push your brother!
common_mistakes: Pushing a door that says 'pull'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Push = away from you. Pull = towards you.

simple_explanation: To use force to move something toward you.
daily_life_use: Opening drawers, dragging objects.
examples: Pull the door to open it.
She pulled him out of the water.
common_mistakes: Confusing push and pull on doors.
hindi_confusion_correction: Pull is always motion towards your own body.

simple_explanation: To move something upward.
daily_life_use: Picking up bags, boxes, or children.
examples: He lifted the heavy weights.
Lift your arms.
common_mistakes: Saying 'pick up' when it involves serious heavy lifting.
hindi_confusion_correction: Pick up is casual (like a pencil), lift involves more effort or weight.

simple_explanation: To let something fall accidentally or intentionally.
daily_life_use: Losing grip on an object.
examples: She dropped her keys.
Please drop me at the station. (meaning drop off)
common_mistakes: Saying 'I fell the glass' instead of 'I dropped the glass'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Things fall (à¤—à¤¿à¤°à¤¨à¤¾), but people drop things (à¤—à¤¿à¤°à¤¾à¤¨à¤¾).

simple_explanation: To have something in your hands or arms.
daily_life_use: Carrying bags, holding hands.
examples: Can you hold my bag?
They were holding hands.
common_mistakes: Using 'catch' when 'hold' is meant. You catch a moving ball, you hold a still cup.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi, 'à¤ªà¤•à¤¡à¤¼à¤¨à¤¾' is used for both catch and hold. In English, they are strictly different.

simple_explanation: To grab and hold something that is moving through the air.
daily_life_use: Playing sports, taking a bus or train.
natural_native_use: Catch a bus. / Catch a cold.
examples: He caught the ball easily.
I need to hurry to catch the train.
common_mistakes: Using 'catch' for stationary objects.
hindi_confusion_correction: Only use catch for things in motion (a ball, a train) or illnesses.

simple_explanation: To send something through the air with force using your arm.
daily_life_use: Disposing of trash, playing catch.
examples: Throw the garbage in the bin.
He threw the ball to his friend.
common_mistakes: Confusing throw and drop. Drop is just letting go, throw involves force.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤«à¥‡à¤‚à¤•à¤¨à¤¾ = throw. à¤›à¥‹à¤¡à¤¼à¤¨à¤¾ = let go / drop.

simple_explanation: To hold something and take it from one place to another.
daily_life_use: Carrying bags from grocery store, carrying a baby.
examples: He is carrying a heavy suitcase.
Let me carry that for you.
common_mistakes: Using 'take' when 'carry' is more specific about physical burden.
hindi_confusion_correction: Carry emphasizes the physical effort of holding while moving.

simple_explanation: To operate and control the direction of a car or other vehicle.
daily_life_use: Commuting to work, taking a road trip.
examples: She drives to work every day.
Can you drive a manual car?
common_mistakes: Saying 'drive a bicycle'. You 'ride' a bicycle or a bike.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'à¤šà¤²à¤¾à¤¨à¤¾' applies to bikes and cars. In English: Drive a car, Ride a bike.

simple_explanation: To sit on and control a bicycle, motorcycle, or animal.
daily_life_use: Riding a bike for exercise or commuting.
examples: He likes to ride his motorcycle.
She rode a horse on vacation.
common_mistakes: Saying 'drive a bike' instead of 'ride a bike'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Ride is for things you sit ON top of (bike, horse). Drive is for things you sit INSIDE (car).

simple_explanation: To say words in order to exchange information or ideas with someone.
daily_life_use: Having a conversation with a friend or colleague.
examples: She was talking on the phone.
They talked for hours.
common_mistakes: Saying 'discuss about' instead of 'talk about'. Discuss takes no preposition.
hindi_confusion_correction: Talk is conversational and usually implies two-way communication.

simple_explanation: To use your voice to say words, often used for languages or formal situations.
daily_life_use: Giving a presentation or mentioning what language you use.
natural_native_use: Can I speak to John? (on the phone)
examples: He speaks three languages.
The manager will speak to the team.
common_mistakes: Saying 'say English' instead of 'speak English'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Speak is used for languages and one-way formal communication. Talk is for informal conversation.

simple_explanation: To pay attention to a sound or to what someone is saying.
daily_life_use: Enjoying music or paying attention in class.
natural_native_use: Hear me out. / Have a listen.
examples: I love to listen to music.
She wasn't listening to the teacher.
common_mistakes: Saying 'listen music' instead of 'listen to music'. Always use 'to'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Listen is an active choice. Hear is passive (happens automatically).

simple_explanation: To become aware of sound through your ears without actively trying.
daily_life_use: Noticing a loud noise outside.
natural_native_use: I can't hear you over the noise.
examples: I heard a loud bang.
Can you hear me?
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am hearing you' instead of 'I hear you' (it's a non-continuous verb usually).
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'à¤¸à¥à¤¨à¤¨à¤¾' is used for both listen and hear. English separates them by intent.

simple_explanation: To direct your eyes in order to see.
daily_life_use: Searching for items or paying attention to visuals.
examples: Look at the stars.
I am looking for my keys.
common_mistakes: Saying 'look the picture' instead of 'look at the picture'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Look is intentional (like listen). See is automatic (like hear).

simple_explanation: To notice or become aware of someone or something by using your eyes.
daily_life_use: Noticing things around you.
natural_native_use: I see what you mean. (Meaning I understand)
examples: I can see the mountains from here.
Did you see him yesterday?
common_mistakes: Using 'see' when talking about watching a movie at home (use watch instead).
hindi_confusion_correction: See is passive. You see things just because your eyes are open.

simple_explanation: To look at something for a period of time, especially something that is moving or changing.
daily_life_use: Viewing TV, movies, or sports.
examples: We watched television all evening.
He is watching a football game.
common_mistakes: Saying 'see TV' instead of 'watch TV'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Watch is used for moving pictures (TV, sports, kids playing). Look is for still objects (a photo).

simple_explanation: To look at words or symbols and understand what they mean.
daily_life_use: Reading books, messages, or news.
spoken_use: I read an interesting article today.
examples: She loves to read novels.
He is reading the newspaper.
common_mistakes: Mispronouncing the past tense. Present is 'read' (reed), past is 'read' (red).
hindi_confusion_correction: Study (à¤…à¤§à¥à¤¯à¤¯à¤¨ à¤•à¤°à¤¨à¤¾) means to learn deeply. Read (à¤ªà¤¢à¤¼à¤¨à¤¾) just means to look at words.

simple_explanation: To make marks that represent letters, words, or numbers on a surface.
daily_life_use: Taking notes, sending emails, or doing homework.
natural_native_use: Jot it down. / Drop me a line.
examples: Please write your name here.
She writes poetry in her free time.
common_mistakes: Confusing the spelling of 'write' and 'right'.
hindi_confusion_correction: No major confusion, straightforward translation.

simple_explanation: To move something so that a space is no longer covered or blocked.
daily_life_use: Opening doors, windows, books, or boxes.
examples: Can you open the window?
He opened his book to page 10.
common_mistakes: Saying 'open the light' instead of 'turn on the light'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi, 'à¤–à¥‹à¤²à¤¨à¤¾' is sometimes used for switching on electronics (à¤Ÿà¥€à¤µà¥€ à¤–à¥‹à¤² à¤¦à¥‹), but in English, electronics are 'turned on'.

simple_explanation: To shut something that is open.
daily_life_use: Closing doors, shutting laptops, finishing business hours.
examples: Please close the door behind you.
The shop closes at 9 PM.
common_mistakes: Saying 'close the light' instead of 'turn off the light'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Just like 'open', don't use 'close' for electronics. Use 'turn off' or 'switch off'.

simple_explanation: The top part of your body containing the brain, eyes, nose, and mouth.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about headaches, wearing hats, or nodding.
examples: She shook her head to say no.
Wear a helmet to protect your head.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'head' (physical part) with 'mind' (thoughts).
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤¸à¤¿à¤° = head, à¤¦à¤¿à¤®à¤¾à¤— (à¤¸à¥‹à¤šà¤¨à¥‡ à¤•à¥€ à¤•à¥à¤·à¤®à¤¤à¤¾) = mind.

simple_explanation: The thread-like strands growing on a person's head or body.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about grooming, haircuts, or appearance.
examples: He has dark brown hair.
I am getting my hair cut tomorrow.
common_mistakes: Saying 'hairs' when referring to all the hair on someone's head. 'Hair' is uncountable in this context.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi we say 'à¤®à¥‡à¤°à¥‡ à¤¬à¤¾à¤² à¤•à¤¾à¤²à¥‡ à¤¹à¥ˆà¤‚' (plural), but in English it is 'My hair is black' (singular/uncountable).

simple_explanation: The front part of the head, from the forehead to the chin.
daily_life_use: Used to describe expressions, appearance, or washing up.
natural_native_use: She had a big smile on her face.
examples: I wash my face every morning.
He looked at her face closely.
common_mistakes: None specifically common.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤šà¥‡à¤¹à¤°à¤¾ = face. It can also mean to confront something ('face a problem').

simple_explanation: The organ of the body that gives you the ability to see.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about vision, seeing, or eye color.
natural_native_use: I got something in my eye.
examples: She has beautiful brown eyes.
Close your eyes and go to sleep.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the plural 's' when talking about both eyes.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤†à¤à¤–à¥‡à¤‚ = eyes. Always use plural when referring to both.

simple_explanation: The organ used for hearing on the sides of the head.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about listening, hearing, or earaches.
examples: He whispered something in her ear.
Cover your ears, it's too loud.
common_mistakes: Confusing pronunciation of 'ear' with 'year'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Ear' starts with an 'ee' sound, 'Year' starts with a 'y' sound.

simple_explanation: The part of the face used for breathing and smelling.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about smells, breathing, or having a cold.
examples: This flower smells so good to my nose.
He got hit on the nose.
common_mistakes: None specifically common.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤¨à¤¾à¤• = nose.

simple_explanation: The opening in the face used for eating, speaking, and breathing.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about eating, talking, or oral hygiene.
natural_native_use: Don't speak with your mouth full.
examples: Put the food in your mouth.
She covered her mouth when she laughed.
common_mistakes: Mispronouncing the 'th' sound at the end.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤®à¥à¤à¤¹ is mouth, but 'face' is à¤šà¥‡à¤¹à¤°à¤¾. Don't use 'mouth' when you mean 'face' (e.g., wash your face, not mouth).

simple_explanation: One of the hard white objects in the mouth used for biting and chewing.
daily_life_use: Used for dental hygiene and eating.
examples: My tooth hurts a lot.
I lost my front tooth when I was a kid.
common_mistakes: Using 'tooths' as plural instead of 'teeth'.
hindi_confusion_correction: One à¤¦à¤¾à¤‚à¤¤ is tooth. Multiple à¤¦à¤¾à¤‚à¤¤ are teeth.

simple_explanation: The soft organ inside the mouth used for tasting, swallowing, and speaking.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about taste, eating, or languages.
examples: He stuck his tongue out at the doctor.
English is not my mother tongue.
common_mistakes: Misspelling as 'tounge' or mispronouncing as 'ton-goo'.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤œà¥€à¤­ = tongue. It also means language/dialect (e.g., mother tongue).

simple_explanation: The part of the body connecting the head to the shoulders.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about clothing, jewelry, or physical pain.
natural_native_use: I woke up with a pain in my neck.
examples: She wore a beautiful necklace around her neck.
He grabbed him by the neck.
common_mistakes: None specifically common.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤—à¤²à¤¾ means throat (inside) or neck (outside). In English, 'neck' is outside, 'throat' is inside.

simple_explanation: The joint connecting the arm to the body.
daily_life_use: Used when carrying bags, expressing tiredness, or pain.
emotional_tone: Neutral. Can imply burden ('weight on shoulders').
examples: He carried the heavy bag on his shoulder.
She shrugged her shoulders.
common_mistakes: None specifically common.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤•à¤‚à¤§à¤¾ = shoulder.

simple_explanation: The upper limb of the body, from the shoulder to the hand.
daily_life_use: Used for actions like lifting, hugging, or describing injuries.
examples: He broke his arm playing football.
She wrapped her arms around him.
common_mistakes: Using 'hand' instead of 'arm' to describe the whole limb.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi, 'à¤¹à¤¾à¤¥' means both arm and hand. In English, 'arm' is shoulder to wrist, and 'hand' is wrist to fingers.

simple_explanation: The end part of the arm, containing fingers and a thumb, used for holding things.
daily_life_use: Very common. Used for touching, holding, or gesturing.
examples: She held the cup in her hand.
Please raise your hand to ask a question.
common_mistakes: Saying 'hand' when meaning the whole 'arm'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hand is only the part with fingers. The rest is the arm.

simple_explanation: One of the five separate parts at the end of the hand.
daily_life_use: Used when pointing, touching, or wearing rings.
natural_native_use: I cut my finger while cooking.
examples: He pointed his finger at the door.
She wore a ring on her ring finger.
common_mistakes: Calling toes 'fingers'.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤ªà¥ˆà¤° à¤•à¥€ à¤‰à¤‚à¤—à¤²à¥€ is 'toe', à¤¹à¤¾à¤¥ à¤•à¥€ à¤‰à¤‚à¤—à¤²à¥€ is 'finger'.

simple_explanation: The front of the body between the neck and the stomach.
daily_life_use: Used regarding breathing, heart, or medical issues.
examples: The doctor listened to my chest with a stethoscope.
He crossed his arms over his chest.
common_mistakes: None specifically common.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤¸à¥€à¤¨à¤¾ = chest.

simple_explanation: The organ in the body where food is digested.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about hunger, eating, or feeling sick.
examples: Don't take this medicine on an empty stomach.
Eating too much junk food will upset your stomach.
common_mistakes: Mispronouncing the 'ch' as 'ch' (à¤š) instead of 'k' (à¤•).
hindi_confusion_correction: Pronounce it 'sta-mak', not 'sta-mach'.

simple_explanation: The rear part of the human body from the shoulders to the hips.
daily_life_use: Used for posture, carrying bags, or pain.
examples: Keep your back straight when you sit.
He carried the heavy box on his back.
common_mistakes: None specifically common.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤ªà¥€à¤  is 'back'. It also means 'behind' or 'return' in different contexts.

simple_explanation: One of the lower limbs of the body used for walking and standing.
daily_life_use: Used when walking, running, or sitting.
examples: I have been standing all day, my legs hurt.
She sat with her legs crossed.
common_mistakes: Using 'leg' when meaning just the 'foot'.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤Ÿà¤¾à¤‚à¤— = leg (thigh to ankle), à¤ªà¥ˆà¤° = foot (ankle downwards).

simple_explanation: The joint in the middle of the leg.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about bending, sitting, or joint pain.
natural_native_use: My knees hurt when I climb stairs.
examples: He fell and scraped his knee.
The doctor checked his left knee.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing the 'k'.
hindi_confusion_correction: The 'k' is completely silent. Just say 'à¤¨à¥€'.

simple_explanation: The part of the leg that touches the ground when you stand.
daily_life_use: Used regarding walking, shoes, or kicking.
natural_native_use: I have a blister on my foot.
examples: I dropped a heavy book on my foot.
He goes to school on foot.
common_mistakes: Using 'foots' instead of 'feet' for plural.
hindi_confusion_correction: One à¤ªà¥ˆà¤° is foot. Two à¤ªà¥ˆà¤° are feet.

simple_explanation: The hard white pieces that form the skeleton of the body.
daily_life_use: Used in medical situations, injuries, or describing thinness.
natural_native_use: Milk is good for your bones.
examples: The X-ray showed a broken bone.
Dogs love to chew on bones.
common_mistakes: None specifically common.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤¹à¤¡à¥à¤¡à¥€ = bone.

simple_explanation: The red liquid that flows through the bodies of humans and animals.
daily_life_use: Used in injuries, medical tests, or hospitals.
natural_native_use: She faints at the sight of blood.
examples: The doctor took some blood for a test.
He lost a lot of blood in the accident.
common_mistakes: Mispronouncing as 'bload' instead of 'blud'.
hindi_confusion_correction: à¤–à¥‚à¤¨ = blood.

simple_explanation: The organ in your chest that pumps blood through your body.
daily_life_use: Used for the physical organ and for emotions (love, kindness).
examples: Regular exercise is good for your heart.
She has a very kind heart.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing as 'hurt' instead of 'haart'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hurt means 'à¤šà¥‹à¤Ÿ', Heart means 'à¤¦à¤¿à¤²'. The pronunciation is different.

simple_explanation: The organ inside the head that controls thought, memory, and feeling.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about thinking, intelligence, or head injuries.
natural_native_use: The brain needs rest to function properly.
examples: The brain is the most complex organ in the body.
He is the brain behind this project (smartest person).
common_mistakes: None specifically common.

simple_explanation: Feeling good, pleased, or glad.

simple_explanation: Feeling very happy and enthusiastic about something.

simple_explanation: Feeling worried or slightly afraid about what might happen.

simple_explanation: Feeling afraid or fearful.

simple_explanation: Unable to understand or think clearly.

simple_explanation: In need of rest or sleep.

simple_explanation: Feeling unhappy because something is not interesting.

simple_explanation: Feeling or showing wonder because of something unexpected.

simple_explanation: Feeling very pleased and satisfied about something you or someone else has done.

simple_explanation: Not showing or feeling nervousness, anger, or other strong emotions.

hindi_meaning: à¤¨à¤¿à¤°à¤¾à¤¶ à¤”à¤° à¤•à¥à¤°à¥‹à¤§à¤¿à¤¤ / à¤•à¥à¤‚à¤ à¤¿à¤¤ (Niraash aur krodhit / Kunthit)
simple_explanation: Feeling annoyed or less confident because you cannot achieve what you want.

simple_explanation: Sad because one has no friends or company.

simple_explanation: Feeling bad because you have done something wrong.

simple_explanation: Surprised and upset.

simple_explanation: Free from tension and anxiety.

simple_explanation: Slightly angry or irritated.

simple_explanation: Sad or displeased because someone or something has failed to fulfill one's hopes or expectations.

simple_explanation: Experiencing mental or emotional pressure.

simple_explanation: Feeling sure of yourself and your abilities.

simple_explanation: No longer feeling distressed or anxious.

simple_explanation: Feeling emotional pain or sadness.

simple_explanation: Eager to know or learn something.

simple_explanation: Greatly surprised; astonished.

simple_explanation: In a state of general unhappiness or despondency.

simple_explanation: Unhappy, disappointed, or worried.

simple_explanation: Finding something funny or entertaining.

examples: She gave an amused smile.

hindi_confusion_correction: 'Amused' means you find it funny/entertaining. 'Amazing' means wonderful/surprising.
formal_informal_note: Slightly formal but used in everyday speech.

hindi_meaning: à¤¬à¥à¤°à¥€ à¤¤à¤°à¤¹ à¤¥à¤•à¤¾ à¤¹à¥à¤† (Buri tarah thaka hua)
simple_explanation: Very tired.
daily_life_use: I was exhausted after running the marathon.
spoken_use: I'm completely exhausted, I need to sleep.
examples: The exhausted workers went home.
She felt exhausted but happy.
common_mistakes: Saying 'very exhausted'. It's better to say 'completely exhausted'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Exhausted' already means 'very tired', so 'very exhausted' is redundant.

simple_explanation: Feeling sudden, overwhelming fear or terror.
daily_life_use: She panicked when she couldn't find her passport.
spoken_use: I panicked and completely forgot what to say.
examples: The panicked crowd ran towards the exit.
He sounded panicked on the phone.
common_mistakes: Spelling it as 'paniced'.
hindi_confusion_correction: When adding '-ed' to panic, you must add a 'k': panicked.

simple_explanation: Content or pleased with what has been done or what has happened.
daily_life_use: I am satisfied with the results of the test.
examples: He gave a satisfied smile.
We are satisfied with their service.
common_mistakes: Saying 'satisfied from' instead of 'satisfied with'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi we say 'à¤‡à¤¸à¤¸à¥‡ à¤¸à¤‚à¤¤à¥à¤·à¥à¤Ÿ' (satisfied from this), but English uses 'with'.

simple_explanation: Wretchedly unhappy or uncomfortable.
daily_life_use: He has been feeling miserable due to his heavy cold.
examples: She had a miserable time at the party.
The wet weather made everyone miserable.
common_mistakes: Mispronouncing the word with four hard syllables (mi-ser-a-ble).
hindi_confusion_correction: It is usually pronounced smoothly with three syllables: MIZ-ruh-bul.

definition: Feeling or showing pleasure or contentment.
examples: I am so happy to see you.

definition: Feeling, expressing, or causing great pleasure and happiness.
examples: It was a joyful occasion.

definition: Feeling or expressing overwhelming happiness or joyful excitement.
examples: She was ecstatic when she heard the news.

definition: Make (someone) ecstatically happy.
examples: He felt elated at the prospect of a holiday.

definition: Noticeably happy and optimistic.
examples: She greeted him with a cheerful smile.

definition: Feeling or showing great pleasure.
examples: We are delighted to have you here.

definition: Feeling pleasure or happiness.
examples: I am glad you came.

definition: Characterized by or feeling intense excitement and happiness.
examples: The team was euphoric after their victory.

definition: In a state of peaceful happiness.
examples: He seemed content with his life.

definition: Extremely pleased and excited.
examples: I'm thrilled to be invited.

definition: Feeling or expressing great happiness and triumph.
examples: The fans were jubilant after the final whistle.

definition: Extremely happy; full of joy.
examples: We spent a blissful week on the island.

definition: Exuberantly or triumphantly joyful.
examples: She gave a gleeful chuckle.

definition: Clearly emanating great joy, love, or health.
examples: She was radiant with happiness.

definition: Cheerful and lively.
examples: He wished everyone a merry Christmas.

definition: Feeling or showing sorrow; unhappy.
examples: The movie had a very sad ending.

definition: Feeling or showing grief.
examples: She looked at him with sorrowful eyes.

definition: In a state of general unhappiness or despondency.
examples: He felt lonely and depressed.

definition: Feeling or expressing pensive sadness.
examples: She felt a little melancholy at the thought of leaving.

definition: Wretchedly unhappy or uncomfortable.
examples: The cold weather made everyone miserable.

definition: Causing or feeling depression or despondency.
examples: He fell into a gloomy mood.

definition: Suffering from overwhelming distress.
examples: He was heartbroken when she left him.

definition: Extremely shocked and saddened.
examples: They were devastated by the bad news.

definition: In low spirits from loss of hope or courage.
examples: She grew despondent as the search continued without success.

definition: Feeling, expressing, or inducing sadness, regret, or grief.
examples: A mournful breeze blew through the trees.

definition: Overcome with deep sorrow.
examples: The grief-stricken family stood in silence.

definition: Feeling despondent.
examples: He looked downcast after the failure.

definition: Having lost enthusiasm and hope.
examples: The team was dispirited by the continuous losses.

definition: Pitifully sad and abandoned or lonely.
examples: She looked a forlorn figure standing at the bus stop.

definition: Oppressively solemn or sober in mood.
examples: He looked at her with a somber expression.

definition: Feeling or showing strong annoyance, displeasure, or hostility.
examples: She felt angry about the injustice.

definition: Extremely angry.
examples: He was furious when he found out the truth.

definition: Very angry; furious.
examples: The enraged customer demanded a refund.

definition: Showing or feeling slight anger; annoyed.
examples: She was visibly irritated by his constant interruptions.

definition: Slightly angry; irritated.
examples: I was annoyed that they didn't call me.

definition: Intensely irritated and frustrated.
examples: She let out an exasperated sigh.

definition: Feeling or expressing bitterness or indignation at having been treated unfairly.
examples: He was resentful of his brother's success.

definition: Aroused to fierce anger, indignation, or resentment.
examples: The public was outraged by the scandal.

definition: Feeling or characterized by great anger.
examples: An irate customer demanded to speak to the manager.

definition: Feeling or showing anger or annoyance at what is perceived as unfair treatment.
examples: She was indignant at being the object of suspicion.

definition: Furiously angry.
examples: He was livid when he saw the damage to his car.

definition: Feeling, showing, or expressing great anger.
examples: She sat in the car, fuming about his comments.

definition: Full of or characterized by intense anger.
examples: The wrathful king ordered the execution.

definition: Feeling or showing anger, hurt, or resentment because of bad experiences or a sense of unjust treatment.
examples: She remained bitter about the divorce.

definition: Annoyed or angry.
examples: He was cross that they were late.

definition: Feeling fear or anxiety; frightened.
examples: Are you afraid of spiders?

definition: Fearful; frightened.
examples: I was too scared to look down.

definition: Afraid or anxious.
examples: The frightened child clung to his mother.

definition: Cause to feel extreme fear.
examples: She was terrified of the thunder.

definition: Experiencing worry, unease, or nervousness, typically about an imminent event.
examples: She was anxious about the exam.

definition: Easily agitated or alarmed; tending to be anxious.
examples: He felt nervous before giving the speech.

definition: Feeling sudden, unreasoning terror.
examples: The panicked crowd rushed for the exits.

definition: Anxious or fearful that something bad or unpleasant will happen.
examples: He felt apprehensive about going home.

definition: Anxious or troubled about actual or potential problems.
examples: I'm worried about his health.

definition: So frightened that one is unable to move; terrified.
examples: She looked at him with a petrified expression.

definition: Feeling afraid; showing fear or anxiety.
examples: They are fearful of the future.

definition: Cause someone to feel frightened, disturbed, or in danger.
examples: He was alarmed by the sudden noise.

definition: Frighten or overawe (someone), especially in order to make them do what one wants.
examples: She felt intimidated by the older students.

definition: Causing or feeling anxiety; troubled or uncomfortable.
examples: She felt a little uneasy about the plan.

definition: Filled with horror; extremely shocked.
examples: We were horrified by the news.

definition: Feeling or showing surprise because something unexpected has happened.
examples: I was surprised to see him there.

definition: Greatly surprised; astounded.
examples: She was amazed at his skill.

definition: Greatly surprised or impressed; amazed.
examples: He looked astonished at the news.

definition: Shock or greatly surprise.
examples: I was astounded by the results.

definition: Cause (someone) to feel surprised and upset.
examples: She was shocked by his rudeness.

definition: So shocked that one is temporarily unable to react; astonished.
examples: The audience sat in stunned silence.

definition: Unable to speak, especially as the temporary result of shock or some strong emotion.
examples: The beauty of the landscape left her speechless.

definition: Feeling or showing sudden shock or alarm.
examples: He gave a startled jump.

definition: Perplexed and confused; very puzzled.
examples: He looked bewildered by the instructions.

definition: Greatly astonish or amaze.
examples: They were dumbfounded by the announcement.

definition: Surprise (someone) greatly; astonish.
examples: I was flabbergasted when she told me.

definition: Filled with or revealing awe.
examples: People were awestruck by the sheer size of the monument.

definition: Feeling or showing revulsion or strong disapproval.
examples: He looked disgusted by the mess.

definition: Driven back or repelled, experiencing intense distaste.
examples: She was repulsed by the smell.

definition: Cause to feel disgust.
examples: He was revolted by the cruelty of the act.

definition: Greatly dismayed or horrified.
examples: We were appalled by the living conditions.

definition: Make (someone) feel disgusted or appalled.
examples: I am sickened by this violence.

definition: Make (someone) feel sick; affect with nausea.
examples: The smell of the food nauseated him.

definition: Resentful or annoyed, typically as a result of a perceived insult.
examples: She looked offended by the joke.

definition: Feeling or inspiring optimism about a future event.
examples: He remained hopeful that they would succeed.

definition: Culpable of or responsible for a specified wrongdoing.
examples: She felt guilty about not visiting her parents.

simple_explanation: Used as a greeting when you meet someone or answer the phone.
daily_life_use: Starting a conversation or greeting friends and colleagues.
examples: Hello, nice to meet you.
Say hello to your family for me.
common_mistakes: Saying 'hello' multiple times to keep a conversation going instead of asking a question.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi, 'à¤¨à¤®à¤¸à¥à¤¤à¥‡' is formal. In English, 'hello' can be used in both formal and informal settings.
formal_informal_note: Can be formal or informal. 'Hi' or 'Hey' is more informal.

simple_explanation: Used when leaving or at the end of a conversation.
daily_life_use: Ending a phone call or leaving a place.
examples: It's time to say goodbye.
She waved goodbye as the train left.
common_mistakes: Saying 'goodbye' can sound very final; native speakers often just say 'bye' or 'see you'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'à¤«à¤¿à¤° à¤®à¤¿à¤²à¥‡à¤‚à¤—à¥‡' is common; the English equivalent is 'see you later'.

simple_explanation: Used to give a positive answer or agree with something.
daily_life_use: Confirming information or accepting offers.
examples: Are you ready? Yes.
Yes, that's exactly right.
common_mistakes: Overusing 'yes' in conversation instead of 'yeah' or 'uh-huh', making it sound too formal.
hindi_confusion_correction: Straightforward translation, but 'yeah' is much more common in casual English.
formal_informal_note: Formal. Use 'yeah' or 'yep' in casual settings.

simple_explanation: Used to give a negative answer or refuse something.
daily_life_use: Declining offers or denying a statement.
examples: No, I haven't seen it.
He said no to the offer.
common_mistakes: Saying 'no' too bluntly can sound rude. Often softened with 'I'm sorry, but no'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'à¤œà¥€ à¤¨à¤¹à¥€à¤‚' is polite. In English, 'No, thank you' is the polite form.

simple_explanation: Used to make a polite request.
daily_life_use: Asking for favors or ordering food.
examples: Please close the door.
Water, please.
common_mistakes: Forgetting to say 'please' when asking for things can be considered rude in English.
hindi_confusion_correction: English speakers use 'please' much more frequently than Hindi speakers use 'à¤•à¥ƒà¤ªà¤¯à¤¾'.
formal_informal_note: Expected in both formal and informal situations.

simple_explanation: Used to express gratitude to someone.
daily_life_use: Appreciating someone's help or receiving a gift.
examples: Thanks for coming.
I just want to say thanks.
common_mistakes: Saying 'thanks you' instead of 'thank you' or 'thanks'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Thanks = à¤¶à¥à¤•à¥à¤°à¤¿à¤¯à¤¾ (casual), Thank you = à¤§à¤¨à¥à¤¯à¤µà¤¾à¤¦ (more formal).

simple_explanation: Used to apologize or express regret.
daily_life_use: Apologizing for a mistake or interrupting someone.
examples: I'm sorry I'm late.
Sorry to bother you.
common_mistakes: Using 'excuse me' when you did something wrong. 'Sorry' is for mistakes, 'excuse me' is to get attention.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'à¤®à¤¾à¤«à¤¼ à¤•à¤°à¤¨à¤¾' covers both. In English, use 'sorry' when you are at fault.

simple_explanation: Used to politely get someone's attention or ask them to move.
daily_life_use: Getting someone's attention in public or passing by someone.
spoken_use: Excuse me, where is the station?
examples: Excuse me, could you move?
He made an excuse for being late.
common_mistakes: Using 'sorry' to get attention instead of 'excuse me'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Use 'excuse me' before you bother someone, use 'sorry' after you bother them.
formal_informal_note: Polite and appropriate for all settings.

simple_explanation: Used to greet someone arriving or as a polite response to 'thank you'.
daily_life_use: Greeting guests or replying to gratitude.
examples: Welcome to our home.
Thanks for the help. - You're welcome.
common_mistakes: Saying 'welcome' when handing something to someone (use 'here you go' instead).
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'à¤¸à¥à¤µà¤¾à¤—à¤¤' is only for receiving guests. In English 'welcome' is also the reply to 'thank you'.

simple_explanation: Used to say yes enthusiastically or to express certainty.
daily_life_use: Agreeing to requests or confirming something.
examples: Are you sure?
Sure, let's do it.
common_mistakes: Using 'sure' when you actually mean 'maybe', which can confuse native speakers.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Sure' is a stronger 'yes' or 'I agree' compared to 'à¤¹à¤¾à¤'.

simple_explanation: Used to agree, express understanding, or check if something is fine.
daily_life_use: Confirming plans or indicating everything is alright.
examples: Are you feeling okay?
Okay, I understand.
common_mistakes: Overusing 'okay' in every sentence as a filler word.
hindi_confusion_correction: Perfect translation for 'à¤ à¥€à¤• à¤¹à¥ˆ'.
formal_informal_note: Neutral, can be used in almost any context.

simple_explanation: Similar to okay; meaning acceptable or in good condition.

simple_explanation: Used to express strong surprise or admiration.
daily_life_use: Reacting to surprising news or seeing something beautiful.
examples: Wow, I didn't know that!
Wow, what a great car.
common_mistakes: Using it sarcastically when a genuine reaction is expected.
hindi_confusion_correction: Exact equivalent of 'à¤µà¤¾à¤¹!'.

simple_explanation: Used to emphasize a point or express surprise/doubt.
daily_life_use: Asking for confirmation or exaggerating an adjective.

Really? I didn't know that.
common_mistakes: Using 'really' instead of 'very' repeatedly (e.g., 'really really good').

simple_explanation: Used to emphasize that something is completely correct.

hindi_confusion_correction: Often used as a one-word answer to mean 'à¤¬à¤¿à¤²à¥à¤•à¥à¤² à¤¸à¤¹à¥€ à¤¬à¤¾à¤¤'.

simple_explanation: Used to say 'yes' strongly or to emphasize that something is completely true.

simple_explanation: Used to indicate that something is possible or that you are not sure.

Are you coming? Maybe.

simple_explanation: Used to mean that something is very likely to happen or be true.

simple_explanation: Without any doubt.

simple_explanation: In a way that is easily seen or understood.

hindi_confusion_correction: Similar to 'à¤œà¤¼à¤¾à¤¹à¤¿à¤° à¤¸à¥€ à¤¬à¤¾à¤¤ à¤¹à¥ˆ'. Use with care so you don't sound rude.

hindi_meaning: à¤ˆà¤®à¤¾à¤¨à¤¦à¤¾à¤°à¥€ à¤¸à¥‡ / à¤¸à¤š à¤•à¤¹à¥‚à¤ à¤¤à¥‹ (imaandari se / sach kahoon toh)
simple_explanation: Used to emphasize that you are telling the truth or your true opinion.
daily_life_use: Sharing a personal opinion frankly.

simple_explanation: In a serious way, or used to express surprise and ask if something is true.

simple_explanation: Used to state the truth, especially when it is different from what was expected.

simple_explanation: Used to summarize or explain something in a simple way.

simple_explanation: Completely, entirely.

simple_explanation: In every way or as much as possible.

simple_explanation: Anything or everything; also used to say you don't care.

simple_explanation: Used to change the subject, return to a previous subject, or end a conversation.

simple_explanation: Morally good, factual, or a direction.

simple_explanation: Not correct or not true.

simple_explanation: In accordance with fact or reality.

simple_explanation: Not true or not real.

simple_explanation: To be desired or approved of; of high quality.

simple_explanation: Of poor quality or low standard; unacceptable.

simple_explanation: Extremely good or impressive.

definition: To seek information or an answer.
examples: Informal: Can I ask you a question?
Formal: May I inquire about the project?
Professional: We request your prompt response.

definition: To acquire something in exchange for money.
examples: Informal: I need to buy some supplies.
Formal: We plan to purchase new equipment.
Professional: The department will procure the necessary assets.

definition: To come into possession of something.
examples: Informal: Did you get the email?
Formal: Please ensure you obtain the required permits.
Professional: The company aims to acquire new talent.

definition: To establish the truth or accuracy of something.
examples: Informal: Let me check the numbers.
Formal: Please verify the details before submission.
Professional: We need to validate the integrity of the data.

definition: To make it easier for someone to do something.
examples: Informal: Can you help me with this task?
Formal: I will assist you with the onboarding process.
Professional: Our goal is to facilitate seamless communication.

definition: To communicate information to someone.
examples: Informal: Tell me when you're ready.
Formal: Please inform the team of your decision.
Professional: We will notify stakeholders regarding the update.

definition: To put into service or apply for a purpose.
examples: Informal: You can use this template.
Formal: We should utilize the available resources effectively.
Professional: The organization will implement the new software framework.

definition: To begin an action or process.
examples: Informal: Let's start the meeting.
Formal: The proceedings will commence shortly.
Professional: The project manager will initiate the deployment phase.

definition: To restore to a sound or working condition.
examples: Informal: Can you fix this bug?
Formal: The technician will repair the machinery.
Professional: We must resolve this issue before the deadline.

definition: To continue to have or hold.
examples: Informal: Keep the document safe.
Formal: The company will retain a copy of the agreement.
Professional: It is imperative that we preserve our market share.

definition: To make something visible or clear.
examples: Informal: Show me how it works.
Formal: The data will demonstrate our progress.
Professional: The case study will illustrate our core competencies.

definition: To make or become different.
examples: Informal: We need to change the plan.
Formal: Please modify the existing protocol.
Professional: We must adapt to the shifting market dynamics.

definition: To form something by putting parts together.
examples: Informal: We can make a new prototype.
Formal: Our factory will manufacture the components.
Professional: The firm strives to produce high-quality deliverables.

definition: To convey an idea or feeling in words.
examples: Informal: I want to say thanks.
Formal: Allow me to express my gratitude.
Professional: He can articulate the project goals clearly.

definition: To present voluntarily and without expecting compensation.
examples: Informal: Give me the report.
Formal: Please provide the necessary documentation.
Professional: We will allocate resources to the new initiative.

definition: To inspect closely or in detail.
examples: Informal: Look at this chart.
Formal: We will examine the evidence.
Professional: The committee will review the proposal tomorrow.

definition: To reflect upon or assess something.
examples: Informal: Think about what you want.
Formal: Please consider the following options.
Professional: We need to evaluate the potential risks.

definition: To discover with certainty.
examples: Informal: Find out if he is coming.
Formal: We must ascertain the facts.
Professional: The audit will determine our financial standing.

definition: To build, arrange, or organize.
examples: Informal: Set up the room for the party.
Formal: The university will establish a new program.
Professional: The IT team will configure the server architecture.

definition: To put together parts or materials.
examples: Informal: They want to build a house.
Formal: The company will construct a new facility.
Professional: We aim to develop a robust software solution.

definition: To allow something to happen.
examples: Informal: Let him go early.
Formal: We cannot permit such behavior.
Professional: The manager will authorize the transaction.

definition: To leave out or fail to include.
examples: Informal: Just drop that idea.
Formal: Please omit my name from the list.
Professional: The policy will exclude any non-compliant practices.

definition: To bear with patience or restraint.
examples: Informal: I can't put up with this noise.
Formal: We will not tolerate infractions.
Professional: The organization must endure challenging market cycles.

definition: To direct attention to something.
examples: Informal: Let me point out the problem.
Formal: The results indicate a positive trend.
Professional: The contract must specify the terms of engagement.

definition: To act in opposition to.
examples: Informal: I don't want to go against them.
Formal: The board will oppose the motion.
Professional: These findings contradict the initial hypothesis.

definition: To mention or introduce a topic.
examples: Informal: Don't bring up the budget.
Formal: I would like to raise a concern.
Professional: The presenter will introduce new methodologies.

definition: To connect or interact with someone.
examples: Informal: Let's get in touch next week.
Formal: Please contact our support team.
Professional: It is vital that we communicate our strategic goals.

definition: To exchange ideas or information.
examples: Informal: We need to talk about the project.
Formal: We will discuss the agenda in the meeting.
Professional: The directors will confer regarding the merger.

definition: To explore or study carefully.
examples: Informal: I'll look into it tomorrow.
Formal: The authorities will investigate the incident.
Professional: The analyst will analyze the current market trends.

definition: To become less or smaller in amount.
examples: Informal: Prices are going down.
Formal: The temperature will decrease overnight.
Professional: The company experienced a decline in quarterly revenue.

definition: To become larger or greater.
examples: Informal: Sales went up this month.
Formal: The population continues to increase.
Professional: Costs may escalate if mitigation is not implemented.

definition: To make understandable or free from doubt.
examples: Informal: Let me clear up the confusion.
Formal: Please clarify your earlier statement.
Professional: We must resolve the pending litigation.

definition: To burst or expand suddenly.
examples: Informal: The balloon blew up.
Formal: The combustible materials can explode.
Professional: Do not magnify minor issues into critical crises.

definition: To abandon or stop an event or arrangement.
examples: Informal: Call off the search.
Formal: We had to cancel the reservation.
Professional: The client elected to terminate the contract.

definition: To delay an action or event.
examples: Informal: Don't put off your homework.
Formal: We must postpone the event due to weather.
Professional: The committee decided to defer the decision.

definition: To fail to include or mention.
examples: Informal: Leave out the unimportant details.
Formal: Please omit this paragraph from the final draft.
Professional: We can disregard anomalous data points.

definition: To cease trying or hand over control.
examples: Informal: Don't give up now.
Formal: The suspect was forced to surrender.
Professional: The outgoing CEO will relinquish control next week.

definition: To solve a problem or understand something.
examples: Informal: I need to figure out the answer.
Formal: The scientists hope to discover a cure.
Professional: The auditor can deduce the origin of the discrepancy.

definition: To deliver or give something for review.
examples: Informal: I handed in my assignment.
Formal: Please submit your application online.
Professional: The firm will present its findings to the board.

definition: To move or happen faster.
examples: Informal: Can we speed up the process?
Formal: We must accelerate production to meet demand.
Professional: Please expedite the shipping process.

definition: To symbolize or mean something.
examples: Informal: What does this symbol stand for?
Formal: The flag is designed to represent our nation.
Professional: These metrics signify an upward trajectory.

definition: To gather or take possession of.
examples: Informal: I need to pick up my bags.
Formal: We will collect the data tomorrow.
Professional: The corporation intends to acquire the startup.

definition: To provide evidence or assistance.
examples: Informal: You need to back up your claims.
Formal: I fully support this initiative.
Professional: We must substantiate these allegations with facts.

definition: To discharge a worker temporarily or permanently.
examples: Informal: The factory had to lay off 50 people.
Formal: The company was forced to dismiss several employees.
Professional: HR will terminate the employment contract effectively.

definition: To refuse to accept or consider.
examples: Informal: They turned down my offer.
Formal: The committee voted to reject the proposal.
Professional: We regretfully decline your invitation.

definition: To examine or evaluate.
examples: Informal: Look over these documents.
Formal: I will review the file tonight.
Professional: We need to assess the overall performance.

definition: To cease to function or physically yield.
examples: Informal: The car broke down.
Formal: The old structure began to collapse.
Professional: The system will fail without proper maintenance.

definition: To finish or complete an activity.
examples: Informal: Let's wrap up this meeting.
Formal: The speaker will conclude his remarks shortly.
Professional: We must finalize the budget by Friday.

definition: To add information to a form or document.
examples: Informal: Fill out this form.
Formal: Please complete the application.
Professional: The script will automatically populate the database.

definition: To organize or fix a problem.
examples: Informal: We need to sort out this mess.
Formal: The mediator will resolve the conflict.
Professional: We need to systematize our inventory tracking.

definition: To reject or dismiss from consideration.
examples: Informal: You can't rule out that option.
Formal: We must exclude irrelevant factors.
Professional: Our strategy should eliminate redundant processes.

definition: To cause something to exist or happen again.
examples: Informal: Bring back the old rules.
Formal: Please return the borrowed items.
Professional: The goal is to restore operational efficiency.

definition: To become involved in a difficult situation.
examples: Informal: I had to step in and stop them.
Formal: The UN decided to intervene.
Professional: Legal counsel will intercede on our behalf.

definition: To begin to understand or realize.
examples: Informal: He finally caught on to the joke.
Formal: I understand the instructions clearly.
Professional: It is difficult to comprehend the full scope of the project.

definition: To look at or evaluate something.
examples: Informal: Check out this new app.
Formal: The police will investigate the crime scene.
Professional: We must meticulously examine the contract.

definition: To hand out or share to multiple recipients.
examples: Informal: They gave out free samples.
Formal: The charity will distribute food.
Professional: The PR team will disseminate the press release.

definition: To separate something into its parts.
examples: Informal: I took apart the engine.
Formal: Workers will dismantle the scaffolding.
Professional: We must deconstruct the theoretical framework.

definition: To create by joining parts.
examples: Informal: I put together a quick meal.
Formal: Please assemble the furniture.
Professional: The analyst will compile the quarterly report.

definition: To get rid of something unwanted.
examples: Informal: Don't throw away that paper.
Formal: You may discard the packaging.
Professional: We must properly dispose of hazardous waste.

definition: To pause or wait for a short time.
examples: Informal: Hold on a second.
Formal: Please wait in the lobby.
Professional: We will delay the launch until next month.

definition: To leave or break free from a location.
examples: Informal: We need to get out of here.
Formal: The prisoner managed to escape.
Professional: The building design ensures safe egress.

definition: To attempt to find something.
examples: Informal: I'm looking for my keys.
Formal: They seek asylum in a new country.
Professional: We will search the database for relevant entries.

definition: To experience or meet unexpectedly.
examples: Informal: I ran into an old friend.
Formal: You may encounter some difficulties.
Professional: The board will meet tomorrow to discuss the issue.

definition: To confirm or establish certainty.
examples: Informal: Make sure the door is locked.
Formal: We must ensure compliance with regulations.
Professional: Please verify the authenticity of the signature.

definition: To start suddenly or strongly.
examples: Informal: A fight broke out in the bar.
Formal: The volcano is expected to erupt.
Professional: New market leaders will emerge this year.

definition: To consume all of something.
examples: Informal: We ran out of milk.
Formal: They will exhaust their supplies soon.
Professional: Overuse will deplete our natural resources.

definition: To bring focus to a specific detail.
examples: Informal: She pointed out my mistake.
Formal: The report will highlight key achievements.
Professional: We must emphasize the importance of cybersecurity.

definition: To restore something to its original owner.
examples: Informal: Give back my book.
Formal: Please return the merchandise within 30 days.
Professional: The company will refund the purchase price.

definition: To take action to resolve or control something.
examples: Informal: I can't deal with this stress.
Formal: She will manage the team effectively.
Professional: The customer service team will handle your inquiry.

definition: To concisely present the main points.
examples: Informal: To sum up, it was a bad idea.
Formal: Please summarize the key findings.
Professional: This document aims to encapsulate our corporate strategy.

definition: To resolve a problem or reach a conclusion.
examples: Informal: We will work out a plan.
Formal: The engineers will solve the mechanical issue.
Professional: The algorithm will calculate the optimal route.

definition: To perform or complete a task.
examples: Informal: We need to carry out the boss's orders.
Formal: The state will execute the mandate.
Professional: We plan to implement the new protocols next quarter.

definition: To recall or reverse a statement or action.
examples: Informal: I take back what I said.
Formal: The newspaper had to retract the article.
Professional: The investor decided to withdraw the funding.

definition: To move away without taking someone or something.
examples: Informal: Don't leave your jacket behind.
Formal: They were forced to abandon the ship.
Professional: We must not forsake our core values.

definition: To criticize or demean.
examples: Informal: Stop putting me down.
Formal: It is unprofessional to insult colleagues.
Professional: The manager should not deprecate employees' efforts.

definition: To reach the same standard or level.
examples: Informal: I need to catch up on sleep.
Formal: The student continues to progress well.
Professional: We must advance our technological capabilities.

definition: To find or encounter by chance.
examples: Informal: I came across an interesting article.
Formal: They hope to discover new species.
Professional: We need to identify potential market opportunities.

definition: To discover the exact place of.
examples: Informal: Help me find my phone.
Formal: We must locate the missing documents.
Professional: Our research aims to discover novel solutions.

definition: To make an effort to do something.
examples: Informal: I will try to be there on time.
Formal: Do not attempt to breach the firewall.
Professional: We will endeavor to exceed client expectations.

definition: To have a requirement for.
examples: Informal: I need a vacation.
Formal: This position will require a bachelor's degree.
Professional: The proposed changes will necessitate a budget review.

definition: Relating to education and scholarship.
examples: She has a strong academic background.

definition: A place of study or training in a special field.
examples: He attends a military academy.

definition: The process or fact of entering or being allowed to enter a place or organization.
examples: She applied for admission to the university.

definition: The part of mathematics in which letters and other general symbols are used to represent numbers and quantities.
examples: He struggles with algebra.

definition: Graduates or former students of a particular school, college, or university.
examples: The university has a large network of alumni.

definition: Detailed examination of the elements or structure of something.
examples: The data requires further analysis.

definition: The branch of mathematics dealing with the properties and manipulation of numbers.
examples: Mental arithmetic is important.

definition: The evaluation or estimation of the nature, quality, or ability of someone or something.
examples: Continuous assessment is used in this course.

definition: A task or piece of work assigned to someone as part of a job or course of study.
examples: She finished her history assignment.

definition: The action or state of going regularly to or being present at a place or event.
examples: Attendance is mandatory for all students.

definition: A person who holds an undergraduate degree from a university or college.
examples: He has a Bachelor of Arts degree.

definition: The study of living organisms.
examples: She is majoring in biology.

definition: A large board with a smooth dark surface used for writing on with chalk.
examples: The teacher wrote the equation on the blackboard.

definition: The branch of mathematics that deals with the finding and properties of derivatives and integrals of functions.
examples: Calculus is a prerequisite for this physics class.

definition: The grounds and buildings of a university or college.
examples: They live in a dorm on campus.

definition: An official document attesting a certain fact.
examples: She received a certificate for completing the course.

definition: The branch of science that deals with the identification of the substances of which matter is composed.
examples: We did an experiment in chemistry class.

definition: A fellow member of a class at school or college.
examples: He is studying with his classmate.

definition: A room, typically in a school, in which a class of students is taught.
examples: The students entered the classroom.

definition: An educational institution or establishment.
examples: She will go to college next year.

definition: A ceremony in which degrees or diplomas are conferred on graduating students.
examples: The commencement speaker was very inspiring.

definition: A creative work, especially a poem or piece of music, or an essay.
examples: Write a composition about your summer holiday.

definition: The action or capability of understanding something.
examples: Reading comprehension is tested in the exam.

definition: Required by law or a rule; obligatory.
examples: Math is a compulsory subject.

definition: A set of classes or a plan of study on a particular subject.
examples: I enrolled in a photography course.

definition: A unit of study counting toward a degree or diploma.
examples: You need 120 credits to graduate.

definition: The subjects comprising a course of study in a school or college.
examples: The school has a very broad curriculum.

definition: The head of a college or university faculty or department.
examples: The dean of the science department resigned.

definition: An academic rank conferred by a college or university after examination or after completion of a course.
examples: She holds a degree in economics.

definition: A division of a large organization such as a university.
examples: He teaches in the history department.

definition: A certificate awarded by an educational establishment to show that someone has successfully completed a course of study.
examples: He received his high school diploma.

definition: A branch of knowledge, typically one studied in higher education.
examples: Sociology is a relatively new discipline.

definition: A long essay on a particular subject, especially one written as a requirement for the Doctor of Philosophy degree.
examples: She is writing her dissertation on ancient history.

definition: A large bedroom for a number of people in a school or institution.
examples: He lives in the boys' dormitory.

definition: The process of receiving or giving systematic instruction, especially at a school or university.
examples: Education is important for everyone.

definition: An optional course of study.
examples: She took art as an elective.

definition: Relating to the basic elements of a subject.
examples: He is an elementary school teacher.

definition: A book or set of books giving information on many subjects or on many aspects of one subject.
examples: She looked up the animal in the encyclopedia.

definition: Officially register as a member of an institution or a student on a course.
examples: He decided to enroll in the program.

definition: The action of enrolling or being enrolled.
examples: Enrollment has increased this year.

definition: A short piece of writing on a particular subject.
examples: The teacher asked us to write an essay on climate change.

definition: The making of a judgment about the amount, number, or value of something; assessment.
examples: The course involves continuous evaluation.

definition: A formal test of a person's knowledge or proficiency in a subject or skill.
examples: The final examination is next week.

definition: A scientific procedure undertaken to make a discovery, test a hypothesis, or demonstrate a known fact.
examples: They conducted a chemistry experiment.

definition: The teaching staff of a university or college.
examples: The university has a distinguished faculty.

definition: A first-year student at a university, college, or high school.
examples: She is a freshman at the university.

definition: The study of the physical features of the earth and its atmosphere.
examples: Geography is my favorite subject.

definition: The branch of mathematics concerned with the properties and relations of points, lines, surfaces, solids, and higher dimensional analogs.
examples: We learned about triangles in geometry.

definition: A mark indicating a student's level of accomplishment.
examples: He got a good grade on his test.

definition: A person who has successfully completed a course of study or training, especially an undergraduate academic degree.
examples: She is a graduate of Harvard University.

definition: The receiving or conferring of an academic degree or diploma.
examples: Her family attended her graduation.

definition: The whole system and structure of a language or of languages in general.
examples: He has poor English grammar.

definition: A school that typically comprises grades 9 through 12, attended after middle school.
examples: He plays basketball for his high school.

definition: The study of past events, particularly in human affairs.
examples: We are learning about Roman history.

definition: Schoolwork that a student is required to do at home.
examples: Have you finished your math homework?

definition: Unable to read or write.
examples: A large percentage of the population is illiterate.

definition: An organization having a particular purpose, especially one that is involved with science, education, or a specific profession.
examples: He works at a research institute.

definition: A person who teaches something.
examples: She is a driving instructor.

definition: The ability to acquire and apply knowledge and skills.
examples: She has a high level of intelligence.

definition: A student in the third year of a course at a US college or high school.
examples: He is a junior in high school.

definition: A school or class that prepares children for first grade.
examples: My daughter goes to kindergarten.

definition: Facts, information, and skills acquired through experience or education.
examples: He has a lot of knowledge about history.

definition: A room or building equipped for scientific experiments, research, or teaching.
examples: We wear safety goggles in the laboratory.

definition: The method of human communication, either spoken or written.
examples: English is my second language.

definition: Gain or acquire knowledge of or skill in something by study, experience, or being taught.
examples: She is learning to play the piano.

definition: An educational talk to an audience, especially one of students in a university.
examples: The professor gave a lecture on economics.

definition: A person who gives lectures, especially as an occupation at a university.
examples: She is a lecturer in biology.

definition: A period of learning or teaching.
examples: Our first lesson is English.

definition: A building or room containing collections of books for use or borrowing.
examples: I borrowed three books from the library.

definition: The ability to read and write.
examples: Adult literacy programs have been successful.

definition: Written works, especially those considered of superior or lasting artistic merit.
examples: She is studying English literature.

definition: A student's principal subject or course of study.
examples: His major is computer science.

definition: The abstract science of number, quantity, and space.
examples: She is very good at mathematics.

definition: Commit to memory; learn by heart.
examples: I have to memorize this poem for tomorrow.

definition: An experienced and trusted adviser.
examples: He has been my mentor for years.

definition: A school for children in the grades between elementary and high school.
examples: She teaches in a middle school.

definition: A student's subsidiary subject or course of study.
examples: She graduated with a major in biology and a minor in chemistry.

definition: A small book with blank or ruled pages for writing notes in.
examples: Write the vocabulary in your notebook.

definition: A thing aimed at or sought; a goal.
examples: The objective of the lesson is to improve pronunciation.

definition: A general description or plan giving the essential features of something but not the detail.
examples: Write an outline before you start your essay.

definition: A distinct section of a piece of writing.
examples: Read the first paragraph aloud.

definition: Be successful in an exam, course, or class.
examples: I hope to pass the exam.

definition: The method and practice of teaching.
examples: He is studying pedagogy at university.

definition: The branch of science concerned with the nature and properties of matter and energy.
examples: We study forces and motion in physics.

definition: The practice of taking someone else's work or ideas and passing them off as one's own.
examples: Plagiarism will result in an automatic failure.

definition: Of chief importance; principal.
examples: The primary objective is to learn the basics.

definition: The most important senior researcher or instructor, or the head of a school.
examples: The principal gave a speech at the assembly.

definition: A university academic of the highest rank.
examples: The professor published a new book.

definition: An individual or collaborative enterprise that is carefully planned to achieve a particular aim.
examples: We are working on a science project.

definition: A student in school.
examples: The school has over 500 pupils.

definition: A pass of an examination or an official completion of a course.
examples: You need the right qualifications for this job.

definition: A test of knowledge, especially a brief, informal test given to students.
examples: We have a pop quiz in math today.
